fragments.content: Drug discovery is the process of identifying potential therapeutic compounds. It begins with target identification—finding a biological molecule (protein, gene) involved in disease pathogenesis. Hit-to-lead involves screening thousands of compounds to find 'hits' that interact with the target, then optimizing them into 'leads' with better potency and selectivity. Preclinical testing evaluates safety, pharmacokinetics, and efficacy in vitro and in animal models before human trials. The process typically takes 3-6 years with high attrition rates.

The drug discovery pipeline follows structured phases: (1) Target validation using genetic, proteomic approaches; (2) High-throughput screening of compound libraries (10,000-100,000+ molecules); (3) Lead optimization improving ADME properties (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion); (4) Preclinical IND-enabling studies including toxicology in two species, pharmacokinetics, and formulation development. Success rate from hit to IND submission is approximately 1 in 5,000-10,000 compounds. Modern approaches include structure-based design, AI-driven discovery, and phenotypic screening.

Preclinical success criteria include: demonstrated mechanism of action at intended target, acceptable safety margin (typically 10-100x exposure over anticipated human dose), favorable pharmacokinetic profile (oral bioavailability >20%, half-life supporting dosing frequency), no genotoxicity or carcinogenicity signals, and scalable synthesis. Failure modes include poor solubility, metabolic instability, off-target toxicity, lack of efficacy in disease models, or inability to achieve therapeutic concentrations. FDA requires GLP-compliant toxicology studies before IND submission.
